page: Google Cloud Platform x4-480-8t-metal is a bare-metal server instance belonging to the x4 server family. It features an x86_64 CPU architecture with 480 dedicated virtual central processing units (vCPUs) and 8192.0 GB of system memory. This configuration yields a memory-to-vCPU ratio of approximately 17.07 GB per core, positioning the server in the memory-optimized hardware class.

The instance does not include local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met through network-attached block storage options. There are no integrated graphics processing units (GPUs) or hardware accelerators present on this server. Additionally, the instance does not include any complimentary public IPv4 addresses, requiring external network configuration for public internet access.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the x4-480-8t-metal provides high resource density, consolidating massive memory arrays and compute cores onto a single bare-metal host. This design minimizes virtualization overhead and is optimized for large-scale enterprise workloads. Suitable applications include large in-memory databases, enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems, and massive data analytics pipelines that require substantial RAM capacity without the need for local physical storage or GPU acceleration.
description: Google Cloud Platform x4-480-8t-metal is a bare-metal server in the x4 family, featuring an x86_64 architecture with 480 dedicated vCPUs and 8192.0 GB of RAM. This configuration provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 17.07 GB per core, making it a memory-optimized platform. The server does not include local storage, requiring network-attached storage solutions, and lacks integrated GPUs. It also provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Qualitatively, the bare-metal design eliminates virtualization overhead, offering high resource density for enterprise workloads. The server is designed for memory-intensive applications such as large in-memory databases, enterprise resource planning systems, and large-scale data processing workloads that require massive memory capacity and high core counts.
